nativeRange.habitat: Shallow dead or silty reefs at depths generally between 1 and 30 m, most commonly encountered at 1–15 m.

diet.notes: Generally best fed large, meaty marine foods such as krill, raw shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish. Has been reported to accept prepared foods relatively readily, and some individuals have been known to feed from an aquarist's hand. Feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food source.

goodWith: large predatory fish of comparable size

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by portioid, 2048x1651. Tack-sharp full-body in-situ shot; vivid turquoise-blue longitudinal lines on golden-olive body with blue-spotted head and blue-edged fins - textbook diagnostic look.
Photo by portioid, 2048x1437. Sharp full-body reef shot showing the wavy blue lines and blue fan-shaped fins; strong color, whole animal in frame.
Photo by Donald Davesne, 1531x1077. Different, bluer individual in-situ; clear blue-lined patterning, whole fish visible - adds color variety.

summary: The Blue Line Grouper is a robust Indo-Pacific ambush predator with vivid blue-striped patterning, widely considered aggressive toward smaller tank mates and likely to consume any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth — making it best suited to large predator displays.
careNotes:
The Blue Line Grouper (Cephalopholis formosa) is a medium-to-large saltwater fish found across shallow reef habitats in the Indo-West Pacific, ranging from western India to the Philippines, north to southern Japan, and south to northern Australia. It is commonly considered an intermediate-care species, due primarily to its size and the water-quality demands that come with a heavy, meaty diet.

In the aquarium, the Blue Line Grouper tends to behave as an ambush predator, spending much of its time near the substrate — tucked under overhangs or inside caves — and emerging to strike at passing prey. It will readily consume any fish or crustacean that fits in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other invertebrates. For this reason, it is generally best kept alongside large, robust tank mates of comparable size, such as large angelfish, moray eels, and triggerfish.

The species is protogynous — a form of sequential hermaphroditism in which individuals begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a group potentially transitioning to male as it matures. Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. It does not possess venomous spines, and its ambush-predator lifestyle makes it an unlikely escape risk.

Although the Blue Line Grouper poses minimal direct threat to corals, it is generally regarded as reef-compatible only with caution: it will readily consume shrimp, small crabs, and other crustaceans, as well as any small fish sharing the tank.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis formosa has not been documented in hobby literature. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group may transition to male. Wild-caught specimens are typically offered for sale as juveniles or subadults.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. In the wild, as a protogynous hermaphrodite, dominant females transition to male as they grow.
